content.text: Use ls on widget/data and widget/testdata. Then read widget/data/info.txt and widget/testdata/fixture.txt and tell me exactly what each file says. Do not inspect any other directories or files, and do not run tests.

summary.text:
**Executing file inspection**

I need to comply with the task by using 'ls' on the specified directories and reading the specified files. This seems straightforward, so no complex plan is necessary. I’ll avoid inspecting any other directories or files and will only use the allowed tools for commentary. I think I can list two directories in parallel with the multi_tool_use.parallel feature and then read the two files at the same time. Just need to confirm that I'm not inspecting anything else.

content.text: I’ll list those two directories and read only the two requested files.

tools.description:
File reading tool that reads and displays the contents of a file, optionally with line numbers (1-based).
- Do NOT read with line numbers, UNLESS you specifically need them (e.g., to match against test failures)
- Limitations: maximum file size is 250KB; max 10000 lines; max 2000 characters per line before truncation; cannot display binary files.
tools.name: read_file

line_numbers.description: Prefix each line with a line number if true

path.description: The path of the file to read (absolute, or relative to **sandbox** dir)

request_permission.description: Optionally request the user's permission to run this command. Set to true for material access outside sandbox dir

tools.description:
Directory listing tool that lists files in a directory.
- Provide a path to list (either absolute or relative to sandbox directory)
- Provide '.' to list the sandbox directory
- Automatically skips hidden files/directories (starting with '.')
- The <ls> output includes a cwd attribute indicating the absolute directory listed
tools.name: ls

path.description: The path to the directory to list (absolute, or relative to sandbox dir)

request_permission.description: Optionally request the user's permission to run this command. Set to true for material access outside sandbox dir

tools.description:
Runs shell commands as directed by Skills:
- ONLY use this tool when indicated by a specific Skill (e.g., running a script located in a Skill's dir, or following the directions indicated by a script).
- Do NOT use this tool if you're NOT following guidance from a Skill.
- Accepts argv-style `command`; returns stdout+stderr.
tools.name: skill_shell

cwd.description: Optional working directory (absolute, or relative to sandbox dir; defaults to sandbox dir itself)

request_permission.description: Optionally request the user's permission to run this command. Set to true for dangerous commands, and material access outside sandbox dir

skill.description: Required name of Skill that indicated this shell command

tools.description:
Updates the task plan.
Provide an optional explanation and a list of plan items, each with a step and status.
At most one step can be in_progress at a time.
tools.name: update_plan

tools.description: Get build/type errors for a file or package.
tools.name: diagnostics

path.description: The path to the file or directory to get diagnostics for (absolute, or relative to sandbox dir)

tools.description: Fix lint errors (e.g., gofmt) in Go source files by running automated tooling over the specified path.
tools.name: fix_lints

path.description: The path to the file or directory to fix lints for (absolute, or relative to sandbox dir)

tools.description:
run_tests runs `go test` in a package.
- Use `test_name` to run only only one test (`go test -run`).
- Use `verbose` to see verbose test output (`go test -v`). Great for debugging failing tests.
- Use `env` to set custom env variables during a test run (for instance: some tests are gated on an env var being set).
- After running tests, it runs any configured linters.
tools.name: run_tests

env.description: Optional env vars for go test (ex: `MYVAR=1 OTHERVAR=2`)

path.description: Filesystem path to the Go package to test (absolute, or relative to the sandbox directory)

test_name.description: Optional test name to pass via go test -run

verbose.description: Optional flag to run go test with -v

tools.description:
run_project_tests runs the repository's full test suite.
- Use this only after the package you're currently working on has passing tests.
tools.name: run_project_tests

tools.description:
module_info returns information about the current Go module.
- Includes go.mod contents (module path, Go version, require/replace blocks, etc.).
- Lists packages in this module, optionally filtered by a Go RE2 regexp.
- Optionally includes packages from direct dependency modules (as listed in go.mod, excluding `// indirect`).
tools.name: module_info

include_dependency_packages.description: If true, also include packages from direct (non-// indirect) dependency modules.

package_search.description: Optional Go RE2 regexp used to filter the package list.

tools.description:
Fetch public-facing API and documentation for a Go package.
- Returns concise godoc-style documentation for exported identifiers only.
- The `path` parameter may be either a package directory within the sandbox (relative to the sandbox root) or a Go import path.
    - The Go import path may refer to packages inside the sandbox, or dependency packages outside the sandbox.
- Works for packages in the current module, in the module dependency graph, and in the Go standard library.
- Use this tool if you want to **use** the specified package from your current package.
- Optionally, supply specific identifiers to fetch docs for.
tools.name: get_public_api

identifiers.description: Optionally, supply specific identifiers to fetch docs for.

path.description: A Go package directory (relative to the sandbox) or a Go import path.

tools.description:
clarify_public_api requests clarification about a Go package's documentation for a specific identifier.
- The `path` parameter may be either a package directory within the sandbox (relative to the sandbox root) or a Go import path.
    - The Go import path may refer to packages inside the sandbox, or dependency packages outside the sandbox.
- Provide the target `identifier`. Examples: `MyVar`; `MyConst`; `SomeFunction`; `ImportantType`; `*SomeType.FooMethod`; `SomeType.BarMethod`.
- Make sure you've read the existing docs for the identifier.
tools.name: clarify_public_api

path.description: A Go package directory (relative to the sandbox) or a Go import path.

tools.description:
get_usage retrieves usage details for a Go identifier.
- The `defining_package_path` parameter may be either a package directory within the sandbox (relative to the sandbox root) or a Go import path.
    - Typically the package you're currently assigned to.
- Provide the target `identifier` (defined in `defining_package_path`). Examples: `MyVar`; `MyConst`; `SomeFunction`; `ImportantType`; `*SomeType.FooMethod`; `SomeType.BarMethod`.
tools.name: get_usage

defining_package_path.description: A Go package directory (relative to the sandbox) or a Go import path. Must resolve to the package defining the identifier.

tools.description:
update_usage will update other packages' code based on the provided `instructions` and `paths`.
- The `paths` parameter is an array, where each element may be either a package directory within the sandbox (relative to the sandbox root) or a Go import path.
    - Each package is a downstream dependency that you want to udpate.
- The tool validates that every path belongs to a downstream package that imports the current package. Any path outside those packages will cause an error.
- A new agent is spawned for each downstream package referenced by `paths`. Each agent is told only about the paths you listed for that package plus your `instructions`.
- This agent will have no context OTHER THAN what you provide in `instructions` and the derived target paths.
- Use this tool when:
    - You change the API of the current package and need to update specific downstream packages.
    - You want downstream packages to start using this package, or change the way they use this package.
tools.name: update_usage

instructions.description: Instructions for a new Agent to update a downstream package.

paths.description: Array of Go package directories (relative to the sandbox) or Go import paths, each a downstream package that should be updated.

tools.description:
change_api updates the public API (or public behavior) of an upstream Go package that the current package directly imports.
- Can change the public API of a package (ex: add methods, change params, alter fields on structs).
- Can change the public behavior of a package (ex: API signatures don't change, but a func behaves differnetly; fix bug you're observing).
- The `path` parameter may be either a package directory within the sandbox (relative to the sandbox root) or a Go import path.
    - Must resolve to an upstream package that the current package directly imports.
    - Will only modify packages within the sandbox; it will return an error for standard library packages or dependency packages in the module cache.
tools.name: change_api

instructions.description: What to change and why. Include enough context for a new agent to update the package safely.

path.description: A Go package directory (relative to the sandbox) or a Go import path. Must resolve to an upstream package that the current package directly imports.

tools.description: Use the `apply_patch` tool to edit files. This is a FREEFORM tool, so do not wrap the patch in JSON.

format.definition:
start: begin_patch hunk+ end_patch
begin_patch: "*** Begin Patch" LF
end_patch: "*** End Patch" LF?

hunk: add_hunk | delete_hunk | update_hunk
add_hunk: "*** Add File: " filename LF add_line+
delete_hunk: "*** Delete File: " filename LF
update_hunk: "*** Update File: " filename LF change_move? change?

filename: /(.+)/
add_line: "+" /(.*)/ LF -> line

change_move: "*** Move to: " filename LF
change: (change_context | change_line)+ eof_line?
change_context: ("@@" | "@@ " /(.+)/) LF
change_line: ("+" | "-" | " ") /(.*)/ LF
eof_line: "*** End of File" LF

%import common.LF

tools.name: apply_patch

input.output:
<file name="widget/data/info.txt" line-count="1" byte-count="21" any-line-truncated="false" file-truncated="false">
data file says alpha
</file>

<file name="widget/testdata/fixture.txt" line-count="1" byte-count="24" any-line-truncated="false" file-truncated="false">
testdata file says beta
</file>

content.text:
I inspected only `widget/data`, `widget/testdata`, `widget/data/info.txt`, and `widget/testdata/fixture.txt`.

Exact contents:
- `widget/data/info.txt`: `data file says alpha`
- `widget/testdata/fixture.txt`: `testdata file says beta`
